source.reliabilityAggregation: Pooled across saved cross-validation test predictions.

datasets.label: Breast Tumor Classification
datasets.plainSummary: A cleaner benchmark where models often stay strong overall, which makes calibration drift especially important to notice.

datasets.label: Heart Disease Classification
datasets.plainSummary: A smaller, noisier benchmark where missingness exposes fragility more quickly and trust signals are easier to see.

models.label: Logistic Regression
models.plainSummary: Often easier to reason about and sometimes gentler on calibration-sensitive metrics.

models.label: Random Forest
models.plainSummary: Often the steadiest overall core model in the canonical benchmark.

models.label: Gradient Boosting
models.plainSummary: Competitive when conditions are mild, but more brittle in harsher missing-data settings.

mechanisms.label: MCAR
mechanisms.plainSummary: Values disappear without depending on the patient state in the present simulation.

mechanisms.label: MAR
mechanisms.plainSummary: Missingness depends on other observed variables, not purely by chance.

mechanisms.label: MNAR
mechanisms.plainSummary: Missingness is tied to the value being hidden in the present construction.

severity.label: mild
severity.summary: Only a limited share of the input is missing, so the trust question is more about subtle drift than collapse.

severity.label: moderate
severity.summary: A meaningful share of the input is missing, so probability quality should matter more visibly.

severity.label: heavy
severity.summary: The model is operating with a substantial information gap, so caution should become part of the story.

severity.label: severe
severity.summary: Nearly half the relevant input is missing, which is the kind of setting where confident language can become misleading.
